topic.what: VIGOR (Vioxx Gastrointestinal Outcomes Research) trial comparing rofecoxib 50 mg vs naproxen 500 mg bid for GI safety in rheumatoid arthritis patients
topic.how: Large-scale randomized controlled trial (n=8076) with confirmed clinical GI endpoints (perforation, symptomatic ulcers, upper GI bleeding) and prospective cardiovascular event adjudication over median 9 months follow-up
topic.why_matters: Demonstrated that COX-2-selective inhibition halved serious GI events compared to traditional NSAIDs but revealed an unexpected increase in cardiovascular thrombotic events, ultimately leading to rofecoxib's global withdrawal in 2004 and reshaping NSAID safety paradigms

characteristics.what_it_measures: Relative reduction in confirmed serious upper GI events (perforation, symptomatic ulcer, bleeding) with rofecoxib vs naproxen
characteristics.vs_baseline: Compared to naproxen 500 mg bid (a non-selective NSAID)
characteristics.context: Rheumatoid arthritis patients not on aspirin; GI benefit was the primary endpoint and clearly met

good_for: Understanding the GI-cardiovascular safety tradeoff of COX-2-selective inhibitors
Historical context for rofecoxib (Vioxx) withdrawal and regulatory response
Evidence base for NSAID risk stratification guidelines
not_for: Current COX-2 inhibitor prescribing (rofecoxib withdrawn from market)
Patients on concurrent low-dose aspirin (excluded from trial)
Osteoarthritis management (trial population was rheumatoid arthritis)
requires: Awareness that rofecoxib dose (50 mg) was supratherapeutic for osteoarthritis

novelty: First large RCT to conclusively demonstrate the GI safety advantage of COX-2-selective inhibition over traditional NSAIDs
Unexpectedly revealed the cardiovascular thrombotic signal of COX-2 inhibition, fundamentally changing the risk-benefit calculus
Triggered the largest pharmaceutical withdrawal in history and transformed drug safety regulation
open_problems: Whether the CV risk was specific to rofecoxib or a class effect of all COX-2 inhibitors remained debated
Role of naproxen's potential cardioprotective effect (via platelet COX-1 inhibition) in inflating the apparent CV difference
Optimal balance point between GI and CV risk for selective vs non-selective NSAIDs remains unresolved
